steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: An astronaut has a mass of 80 kg on Earth. What is their mass on the Moon?
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Mass NEVER changes! Mass is the amount of matter in an object - it stays the same wherever you go. What changes on the Moon is WEIGHT (because g is different). The astronaut’s mass = 80 kg on Earth, on the Moon, and floating in space.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Mass is always the same - it’s the amount of matter in an object. Only weight changes with gravitational field strength. The astronaut’s mass = 80 kg everywhere in the universe.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.5.3: Weight, gravitational field strength

steps.title: Weight and Gravity
steps.bodyHTML:
Weight is a force caused by gravity acting on an object’s mass. It is measured in Newtons (N) using a calibrated spring balance (newtonmeter).

Mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in kilograms (kg). Mass does NOT change with location.

The equation linking them is:
W = m × g

where g is the gravitational field strength, measured in N/kg. On Earth, g ≈ 9.8 N/kg. On the Moon, g ≈ 1.6 N/kg. On Jupiter, g ≈ 25 N/kg.

Because weight depends on g, your weight changes depending on which planet you are on, but your mass stays the same.

relationships: Bigger m → bigger W (if g is constant)
Bigger g → bigger W (if m is constant)
Weight is a FORCE - it acts downwards towards the centre of the Earth

commonErrors: Using g = 10 without checking the mark scheme
Saying weight is the same everywhere

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Choose a planet and drag the mass slider. Watch the weight arrow and scale change in real time. Try Compare mode to see two planets side by side!

aqa.questionText: Calculate the weight of a 65 kg person on Earth. (g = 9.8 N/kg)

edexcel.source: Edexcel style · W = mg on two bodies
edexcel.questionText: A rock has a mass of 4.5 kg. Calculate its weight on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg) and on the Moon (g = 1.6 N/kg).

steps.label: Weight on Earth
steps.content: Use W = m × g with Earth’s g:

steps.label: Weight on Moon
steps.content: Use W = m × g with the Moon’s g:

steps.label: Key point
steps.content: The mass stays the same (4.5 kg). Only the weight changes because g is different.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: units correct on both answers

ocr-21c.questionText: A bag of sugar has a mass of 1 kg. What is its weight? (g = 9.8 N/kg)

steps.label: Apply W = m × g
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: correct equation and substitution; 1 mark: correct answer with unit

aqa.questionText: A student has a mass of 50 kg. Calculate their weight on Earth. (g = 9.8 N/kg)
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark substitution · 1 mark correct answer + unit
aqa.commonMistake: Option A is dividing instead of multiplying. Option D is just restating the mass. confusing mass with weight.

edexcel.source: Edexcel style · W = mg on Moon
edexcel.questionText: An astronaut has a mass of 75 kg. What is their weight on the Moon? (g = 1.6 N/kg)
edexcel.markSchemeAward: 1 mark correct equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit
edexcel.commonMistake: Option B uses Earth’s g (9.8) instead of the Moon’s g (1.6). Always use the g value given in the question.

ocr-gateway.source: OCR Gateway style · rearranging for mass
ocr-gateway.questionText: A box weighs 196 N on Earth. What is its mass? (g = 9.8 N/kg)
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark rearrangement · 1 mark correct answer + unit
ocr-gateway.commonMistake: Option A multiplies instead of dividing. a common error when students forget the rearrangement.

ocr-21c.questionText: A cat has a mass of 4 kg. What is its weight on Earth? (g = 9.8 N/kg)
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit
ocr-21c.commonMistake: Option D just restates the mass as a number of Newtons. mass and weight are not the same!

wjec.questionText: A textbook has a mass of 1.5 kg. Calculate its weight. (g = 9.8 N/kg)
wjec.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit
wjec.commonMistake: Option D uses g = 10 instead of 9.8. always use the g value given in the question.

equation.examTip: Weight is a FORCE. Mass is NOT a force. Always use the g value given in the question - it might not be 9.8.

boardNotes.aqa: On AQA formula sheet. Know all three rearrangements and that weight is measured in Newtons.
boardNotes.edexcel: On Edexcel equation sheet. Must state units for full marks. Remember weight is a force.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: On OCR Gateway formula sheet. Be ready to rearrange for mass or g.
boardNotes.ocr-21c: On OCR 21st Century formula sheet. Understand the difference between mass and weight.
boardNotes.wjec: Provided in WJEC exam. Know all three rearrangements and that g varies by planet.
